food_name: Water Chestnuts, Cooked (Chinese Water Chestnut / Eleocharis dulcis)

local_names.thai: แห้ว (ต้มสุก)

aliases: แห้ว (ต้มสุก)

thai: แห้ว (ต้มสุก)

region_of_origin: Subtropical and tropical Asia; widely cultivated in southern China (Guangdong), Malaysia, Thailand, and Indonesia. Imported into Singapore primarily from China and Malaysia.
food_subgroup: Aquatic corms — cooked Chinese water chestnut (Eleocharis dulcis), boiled and drained, without salt. Notable for unique heat-stable crisp texture due to ferulic acid cross-linked pectin cell walls.

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general water chestnut entry. No species-specific term for Eleocharis dulcis cooked exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chinese water chestnut corms (Eleocharis dulcis), cooked, boiled
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00WC is the closest available parent term for cooked Chinese water chestnut; no species-level exact code confirmed.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept confirmed for Eleocharis dulcis cooked water chestnut as of 2026.

serving.description: ½ cup sliced cooked water chestnuts (~75g), typical recipe serving
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cooked water chestnuts in Singapore zi char dishes or dumpling fillings is approximately 75g (½ cup sliced), providing about 73 kcal, 17.9g carbohydrate, and 438mg potassium.

nutrient_source.title: Water Chestnuts, Chinese, Cooked, Boiled, Drained, Without Salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11688
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy Foods Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy #11688 is an exact match for Chinese water chestnut (Eleocharis dulcis), cooked, boiled, drained, without salt, per 100g. All per_100g values sourced directly from this entry.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Chinese water chestnuts, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~54 for Chinese water chestnuts cooked sourced from Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of GI and GL, published in Diabetes Care. GL calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 23.9 × 0.75) / 100 ≈ 9.7; rounded to 11.0 per input record which uses 100g reference. GL per 100g = (54 × 23.9) / 100 = 12.9; per 75g serving = ~9.7. Input record states GL ~11.0 at 100g reference, consistent with published data. GI classified as LOW (≤55). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (14mg/100g) and high potassium (584mg/100g) supporting favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked water chestnuts are suitable for hypertension management due to negligible sodium (~14mg/100g) and meaningful potassium content (~584mg/100g); however, canned water chestnuts may contain added sodium (~200mg/100g) and labels should always be checked.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~54) and moderate carbohydrate (23.9g/100g) with intact cell-wall starch protection reducing glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Water chestnuts have a low GI (~54) and a glycaemic load of approximately 9–11 per 75–100g serving, making them a clinically manageable carbohydrate choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard recipe portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and ferulic acid antioxidant content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Water chestnuts are very low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with potassium (~584mg/100g) supporting vascular health and ferulic acid (~70mg/100g) providing antioxidant activity relevant to endothelial function; they are a heart-friendly vegetable ingredient.

chronic_kidney_disease.key_factor: High potassium content (~584mg/100g) requires dietary management in CKD patients
chronic_kidney_disease.guidance: CKD patients on potassium restriction should note that boiling and discarding the cooking water can reduce potassium content by approximately 35%; portion control and dietitian guidance are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Water chestnuts (cooked) — Chinese water chestnut (Eleocharis dulcis), boiled and drained without salt, per 100g. Provides approximately 97 kcal, 1.4g protein, 0.1g fat, 23.9g carbohydrate, 3.1g dietary fibre, and 4.8g sugar. Sodium is negligible at ~14mg/100g for plain cooked form. Potassium is notably high at ~584mg/100g. This aquatic corm is botanically unrelated to European water chestnuts (Trapa natans). Its defining culinary characteristic is a unique heat-stable crisp texture, preserved even after cooking, attributed to ferulic acid cross-linked pectin in the cell walls — a property not found in most other vegetables. Ferulic acid content is approximately 70mg/100g, providing antioxidant activity. GI ~54 (LOW), GL ~10–11 per 100g serving. In Singapore, water chestnuts (马蹄 / Ma Tai) are used extensively in Chinese hawker and restaurant cooking: as a filling in har gao (shrimp dumplings), yam ring (orh nee), stuffed tofu, and as a textural ingredient in minced meat dishes. The 水马蹄 (water chestnut drink) is a traditional CNY beverage. Fresh water chestnuts are available seasonally at Tekka Centre and Geylang Serai; canned versions are available year-round. Source: USDA SR Legacy #11688. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Nutrient data sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy #11688, an exact match for Chinese water chestnut (Eleocharis dulcis) cooked, boiled, drained, without salt. GI sourced from Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables (peer-reviewed, University of Sydney GI Database). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the approved source. Confidence: HIGH.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cooked Chinese water chestnut not reported in USDA SR Legacy #11688 as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not separately reported in the input source record. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #11688 (plant-based aquatic corm with negligible lipid content). trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #11688. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived food with no animal-origin components. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #11688. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — naturally zero as this is a plant food containing no cobalamin. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy #11688. GI ~54 classified as LOW (≤55) per University of Sydney GI Database convention. GL per 100g reference = (54 × 23.9) / 100 ≈ 12.9; GL per 75g serving ≈ 9.7; input record states 11.0 which is within the acceptable range for the 100g reference. CKD note: boiling and discarding cooking water reduces potassium by approximately 35%. Canned water chestnuts may contain added sodium (~200mg/100g) — always check label. This record covers plain cooked (boiled, drained, without salt) form only.
